summary: The household gravitates toward classic Hollywood with a strong pull toward pre-1960 films. Film noir from the 1940s is a clear sweet spot — The Third Man was a recent rewatch, and the system has high confidence on that cluster (Carol Reed, Welles-adjacent, postwar European atmosphere). Screwball comedy is the other consistent positive: His Girl Friday, The Thin Man, and anything with fast verbal patter lands well. In TV, the pattern follows — classic sitcoms with strong ensemble comedy (The Dick Van Dyke Show is being watched through steadily). Horror is reliably rejected, especially anything with body-horror or slasher elements; Carnival of Souls was abandoned at 18%. Runtime tolerance is around 130 minutes; three-hour epics go unfinished. No strong preference for musicals or westerns observed yet — treat those as neutral when ranking, probe occasionally. Recent color-era picks (1970s+) show weaker engagement, suggesting the household's taste really does sit pre-1970.
